text.title: UI Library Anti-patterns
text.summary: Common failure modes when building shared UI component libraries: prop explosion, Storybook drift, and barrel-file tree-shaking issues.

antipattern.description: Prop explosion: adding leftIcon, rightIcon, loading, block, round, fullWidth, etc. as separate boolean/node props.
antipattern.reason: Proliferating props create a combinatorial surface that is impossible to test and confusing to use. Use cva() variants and slots (asChild / Slot) instead.

antipattern.description: Storybook drift: shipping components without a *.stories.tsx file, relying on manual inspection.
antipattern.reason: Components without stories cannot be visually tested in CI or reviewed by designers. Agents mark "done" after the component file but skip stories under deadline pressure.

antipattern.description: Barrel file without sideEffects: false in package.json.
antipattern.reason: Bundlers cannot tree-shake named exports from a barrel when sideEffects is missing or true, causing consumers to import the full library even when using one component.

antipattern.description: CSS leakage: styles that affect elements outside the component via unscoped selectors (e.g., button { ... } in a module that uses :global).
antipattern.reason: Leaking styles create invisible coupling between components; adding one component breaks layout in unrelated parts of the consuming app.

antipattern.description: Polymorphic as prop typed as a generic without a pinned PolymorphicProps helper.
antipattern.reason: Generic polymorphic typing breaks on LLM edits; the helper regresses to any, losing ref type safety. Pin a working PolymorphicProps and forbid rewrites.

antipattern.description: Rule of one: extracting a component after the first usage rather than the third.
antipattern.reason: Premature abstraction freezes the API before patterns are understood. Apply the rule of three: extract only after three duplicates exist.
